title: Camera Museum
review_text:
I came across this museum by accident when looking for things to do in London and was not disappointed. It is a must visit for anyone interested in photography. The entry fee is 3 Eur, to be paid after the visit. There is also a cafe and a toilet.
It can also be a fun place for kids who are interested in photography, since they have a few interactive games as well. The museum itself is quite small and you can see everything in 15-20 minutes.

title: Camera Museum
review_text:
After reading several reviews, I visited the Camera Museum, and it exceeded my expectations.
The ground floor houses a stylish café, and you descend a steep staircase, giving it a secretive feel. The museum itself is filled with fascinating historical cameras, and the atmosphere is wonderfully nostalgic.

Although I had a poor connection which delayed my payment for the entrance fee, the staff were incredibly patient and handled the situation with kindness, showing no irritation.

It was a unique and enjoyable experience, especially for photography enthusiasts.

title: Camera Museum
review_text:
Cute, small cafe with a museum of old film cameras in the basement. Reasonably priced coffees and small pastries are served and it costs £3 to visit the museum downstairs.

They also offer some camera repair services and sell old camera parts. It’s a good visit for photography/camera enthusiasts!

title: Camera Museum
review_text:
Yesterday, I attended my graduation, and would you believe that that wasn't even the highlight of my day? No, the highlight was getting to take my parents to the Camera Museum.

For context, my dad spent 35 years as a professional photographer in various different fields. He's now got dementia and we worried that a day in London would be too stressful for him, but the moment we got to the bottom of the stairs at the entrance of the museum (which were a little bit touch-and-go), he suddenly came alive in a way that I haven't seen in at least ten years.

Every single camera brought a twinkle to his eye as he launched into some anecdote about the people he knew through his work or about his training or about his old collection. Who knew a man could talk for so long about his love for the Leica M2? At one point, I think Mum was even getting jealous!

Here I should apologise that we had to remind him almost every five minutes about the "no touching" signs. We had a truly incredible visit that I am sure I shall remember forever. Thank you from the bottom of my heart. Worth every penny.

title: Camera Museum
review_text:
Amazing little museum about cameras, Tucked within the lower level of a charming cafe, this camera museum gives visitors a small snapshot of photographic history. The whole place is a quirky combination of a coffee shop, camera store, and museum.

The lower two rooms are packed with different types of cameras, some of which you can even pick up and hold. The various models basically form a timeline of photographic technology, with cameras dating from the 1800s—the advent of photography—up until the modern digital age.

London's Camera Museum began after one of the cafe’s owners, who happened to be an avid camera collector, decided to open his own small camera shop in the same space where customers enjoyed their coffee and sweets. He later incorporated pieces from his collection into the space, forming the museum.

Even the upstairs cafe shows hints of the collection waiting down below. Photography-themed paraphernalia covers the walls, which patrons can admire while snacking on cookies and cakes. There’s also a small camera repair shop at the site as well.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text:
Very impressive venue. 👏🏻
Friendly and personable staff! Well done.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text:
Visited the Honourable Artillery Company for the London Concours, and I have to say—what an incredible setting for a car show. Tucked away in the heart of the City, the HAC offers a beautiful contrast between historic architecture and the modern luxury cars on display.

The grounds were immaculate, and the event was well-organized. It’s hard to believe such a peaceful, green space exists right in central London. The lineup of vehicles was top-tier, and the setting only added to the prestige of the event. Staff were polite; they handled it smoothly.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text: Attended the London Concours Event for several years at the HAC. Great venue. Buildings with great architecture and historical significance. Apparently the cricket ground is on top of a former plague pit! Fantastic selection of super and hyper cars but decided not to display my cars this year. Recommended.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text:
I recently attended a private event at the Honourable Artillery Company, and it was a remarkable experience. This unique venue offers an incredible blend of historic charm and modern event facilities right in the heart of the City of London. The main event space, Armoury House, exudes grandeur with its high ceilings, wood-panelled rooms, and large windows overlooking the lush Artillery Garden. We enjoyed our gathering in the Long Room, which provides a beautifully historic and refined setting that left a lasting impression on our guests.
One of the standout features is the five-acre Artillery Garden – a rare green oasis amid London's urban landscape. It’s perfect for outdoor events and adds a tranquil atmosphere to the whole experience. The Prince Consort Rooms also provide a more versatile and contemporary setting for larger gatherings, allowing you to adapt the space as needed. Additionally, HAC’s proximity to Moorgate and Old Street stations makes it highly accessible, which is a huge plus.
Overall, the HAC is an outstanding venue choice for any high-profile event, wedding, or corporate gathering. The combination of historic architecture, modern amenities, and stunning outdoor space makes it one of London’s most distinctive and prestigious venues.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text:
A great place that falls under the restricted zone for entry.  One walking in should have a valid ID and a prior appointment.

The place is filled with amazing history of all the war memories and memorabilia.  The medal room speaks volumes of all that people have been recognised with.   So many stories behind those dazzling little precious metals.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text: I attend the London Concours every year, it’s a marvellous venue, right in the heart of the city.

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text: If You get the chance it's a must on Your list. The staff were brilliant. Small museum but the history 🤯

title: Honourable Artillery Company Museum
address: Armoury House, City Rd, London, EC1Y 2BQ
review_text: Hosted by the MOD on the Queen's birthday at the gun salute! Great day. Thank you so much for sharing history and for being so open and honest. Integrity at its finest!!

title: Royal College of Physicians Museum
review_text: The Royal College of Physicians is open to the public and has a small museum. As someone who likes modern architecture I also found the building itself very interesting. The staff were welcoming to casual visitors. I found this by happenstance, being in Regents Park. Ideal for a short visit if you are in the area but in my opinion insufficient of interest to merit a dedicated journey.

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: It’s worth persisting at the entrance to wait for a response to the bell.  The focus of course is on the discovery and research done at this site and not on the considerable work to turn that into a reality.  It’s great that in an area of such pressure for space that the museum remains.  The discovery must’ve led to the saving of many millions of lives.  The museum is small and spread over several floors, but well worth visiting (it’s free).

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: Friendly volunteers. You have to ring the bell and they will buzz you in. You can see the actual room where Penicillin was discovered! A must visit for anyone with an interest in Biology, Mycology, Bacteriology, Science, since this was a discovery of great historical value

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: The Alexandre Fleming Museum is a small but really special place. It tells the story of a doctor and scientist whose work changed the world, in a way that feels personal and easy to connect with. Being located inside a real, working hospital makes the experience even more powerful-you can truly feel the link between his discoveries and everyday medical care. It’s a quiet, thoughtful visit that leaves you with a lot of admiration for both the man and his legacy.

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: A little gem! Tiny but historically significant museum, 15-20 minute visit.  Fleming's small laboratory room, set up as it was when he discovered mould contamination of an agar plate produced a substance that killed bacteria, later to become penicillin working with Oxford University. Also a video and a poster room with more details. Historic and interesting to medical and laboratory workers in particular.  When open (currently M-Th, 10am-1pm, staffed by volunteers), press the buzzer to gain entry, the door is a bit stiff!

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: Fascinating little museum, includes the original laboratory where penicillin was discovered.  Lovely volunteer guide  (Ruth?) 40min visit, good film , definitely worth dropping in.

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text:
A hidden gem! Absolutely worth a visit for anyone interested in history, health, science, or innovation. A visit can be as in-depth or light as you wish depending on how many questions you ask to the wonderful volunteer docents. We very interested in the topic and got a fantastic in depth tour (about 20-30 mins) of the lab from Tianxiao, who was incredibly knowledgeable, passionate, and funny.
There is also a 10 minute movie to watch and a small display room to check out to cement what you’ve learned.

title: Alexander Fleming Museum
review_text: Fantastic museum. Our tour guide Ruth was so friendly and knowledgeable. Highly recommend for a 30 min visit if you are in the area! You get to see Fleming’s lab and a video about the impact of his research.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: Originally a collection within the British Museum in Bloomsbury, it was only in the 1850s that the natural history department’s superintendent Richard Owen pushed for a bespoke space of its own. The result was this grand Romanesque building in South Kensington that took nearly eight years to build and opened in 1881 to great fanfare. Particularly admired is its stunning Hintze Hall, which houses the skeleton of a female blue whale.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: Great place. Very, very good. For the free museum it's just fantastic. Even the big ques were managed very professionaly.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: A huge amount of people confuse this bus stop (sheltered no less) as the actual Natural History Museum. Poor misunderstood bus stop.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: A fabulous building and amazing collection of items to do with the natural world. Very helpful staff. Dinosaurs a bit of a kiddies theme park and had to pay you get into a couple of exhibitions. Cafes good but inevitably crowded and we went after the main rush. Tribes of young school children in high vis jackets wielding clip boards. We found the access good for a powered chair  lifts and loos decent size.  Good trip.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: The building itself is a attraction apart, from outside look like a church, when you get in you will be welcome by a huge pre historical dinosaur skeleton, the place is incredibly big, so think of have at least 2 hours to visit the entire museum.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)
review_text: A center of scientific excellence in the discovery of taxonomy and biodiversity, this world-famous museum promotes the discovery and enjoyment of the natural world through such exciting exhibits as the Life and Earth Galleries, wildlife garden and geological collections.

title: Natural History Museum /Queens Gate (Stop GL)

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text:
A beautiful historic place with a very elegant atmosphere. The house has a special charm and you can truly feel the history inside. The rooms are well preserved and thoughtfully presented, making the visit interesting and enjoyable.
The staff were very friendly and helpful. Indira was especially wonderful — she shared a lot of information about the house and told us many interesting things about the place, which made the visit even more special.

Definitely worth visiting if you are in the area.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text:
We have been trying to get time to visit Handel Hendrix house and finally made it.
Talk about Yin & Yang
In our early years we loved Jimi Hendrix and now in our older years we love Handel & Jimi Hendrix.
What a brilliant house.
So Handel bought a new build 😁 and Hendrix said he knew nothing of the Bloke ( Handel ) but did purchase some of Handels music.
The house has such warmth & character.
Hendrix was more into his music than wealth.
His flat is small , but of the era.
Staff were all so very kind , informative & personable.
A thoroughly enjoyable visit.
Lockers available on lower floor and toilets.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text:
What an incredible experience visiting the Handel & Hendrix House — two iconic neighbours in Mayfair, separated by just a wall but worlds apart in musical history. The museum beautifully captures the lives and legacies of both George Frideric Handel and Jimi Hendrix, and it’s amazing to wander through the rooms where they each lived and created such timeless music.

The staff were exceptionally helpful, both in the museum and in the shop, adding a warm and welcoming touch to the visit. On the day I went, there was live music at separate times — first classical, then rock — which perfectly reflected the spirit of both musicians. Sadly, I didn’t have time to stay and listen, but it added a lovely atmosphere to the house.

You can download a guide which takes you around the exhibits. There's also lockers available for you to put your items in before you start the tour.

If you’re planning to go, I’d recommend arriving early to avoid the crowds and take your time exploring the fascinating exhibits. It’s a truly special place where history, creativity, and music come together under one roof.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text: Really interesting visit to a well preserved London town house that saw two extraordinary composers in two totally different time frames and two different music genres that both influenced music in their own eras. If your lucky you visit when live music is scheduled

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text: This was a really well presented exhibit within the flats that were inhabited by first Handel, and then Hendrix - two very accomplished, yet very different musicians 😃. Interestingly though, even though they lived over two hundred years apart, they essentially used the same space for the same purpose, living and entertaining, both gentleman being very popular in their respective times. The staff here are happy to offer up interesting stories, especially regards Hendrix as we have photographic evidence of how the flat was setup.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text:
Wonderful visit and really friendly staff.
Fascinating history and beautifully presented museum and house. They’ve created a atmospheric and engaging museum with very welcoming staff.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text: Wonderful place for Handel’s lovers to learn about his life, art and a bit of his private life. Very attentive and kind reception and induction of the house. My 11 years old son and I had the chance to listen to the curator about one of his major composition “Messiah”. Highly recommended.

title: Handel Hendrix House
review_text: An interesting and quirky museum that brings together two music legends—Handel and Hendrix—in one unexpected Mayfair townhouse. I really liked the concept; it’s the kind of thing that feels very “London.” Hendrix’s bedroom is a standout, full of 60s character, right down to the ashtray by the bed. The Handel rooms are formal and elegant. Some other parts of the house feel a bit sparse or still in progress, but overall it’s a fascinating and atmospheric visit for anyone into music or curious, offbeat museums.

Everyone we spoke to was friendly and helpful. It is also brilliant that this is free.

I also adore walking along the cobbled street full of converted warehouses to get there.

title: Thames River Police Museum

title: Thames River Police Museum

Rob was fantastic,  gave a great presentation and plenty of stories from his time as an active officer.  We also got to meet serving officers as they came in which added to the experience.
Remember to book in advance as it is by appointment only.

title: Thames River Police Museum
review_text: Eclectic mix of policing from the Thames Police. Lots of artefacts and history crammed into such a small space,  very dated but plenty to read and look at.

title: Thames River Police Museum
review_text: Great place, great Guide! The Guide! English required. You have to make an appointment via email for the tour. I definitely recommend it to everyone! Lots of interesting facts and information about the work of the river police, what they used to do and what they do now.

title: Thames River Police Museum

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text: Spending time with MoH is a balm for the soul. One of the most extraordinary places and group of people I’ve come across. This is not your standard museum experience, it’s a reminder that we are all part of a community where every single person is a human being with a unique and valuable story and who deserves a place to be. It’s an experience and a conversation, rather than a visit and a look at something that belonged to someone else once. If you have ever wondered ‘why’ or ‘who’ about people experiencing homelessness, or why existence sometimes seems so hard in the 21st century, or why quality of life or the natural world seems to be disappearing and wished you could have a glimpse of an answer - come to MoH and you’ll find a different way to see things.

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text:
A highly original and moving cultural experience, using seemingly mundane objects to share authentic and moving stories of tough lives. Staff and volunteers are really friendly, knowledgeable and passionate about the subject.

It's set in an attractive small building full of character, converted sympathetically and surrounded by a beautiful garden. And the free hot drink on arrival was a pleasant surprise.

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text: I visited with a group of social work students to the museum and they and I enjoyed it and got a lot out of it. Booking is essential as it is not a place to just wonder in and view exhibits. The exhibits are presented and brought to life by a story teller who re-tells what the item means to the person who owns it. It offers a unique perspective on homelessness and brings some much needed humanity to it. On arrival we were warmly welcomed by the museum crew who also offered tea and coffee. I would highly recommend a visit.

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text: We were lucky enough to get to experience this wonderful museum as a cohort for a drama school trip! Everyone was so lovely and Lisa did such a wonderful job at retelling the stories of objects through verbatim theatre. People found the stories so compelling and will be taking this into account as inspiration for writing any future pieces. Thank you to all the MOH team!

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text: An incredibly moving and powerful space, the storyteller communicated people’s experiences through their testimonies so beautifully, intense and immersive and full of so much knowledge, possibly the most worthwhile museum visit I have been to as the in-person conversation and presentation of the work this community does was so effective. The loveliest and most welcoming people working and volunteering too.

title: Museum of Homelessness
address: The Manor House Lodge, Seven Sisters Rd, Finsbury Park, London, N4 2DE
review_text:
Invited there as I took part in there last ten years of there projects, open day, and it was a fabulous experience and fun / sociable day out looking forward to attending to more of groups.
Supporting the homelessness community means a lot to, as everyone needs a solid structure around them like a warm,dry home to stay safe,  and private space. Amazing place to visit highly recommend it.😁👍😁👍😁👍

title: Museum Mansion
review_text: The building is spectacular! The map is a little confusing so be sure to check with the helpful information people before trekking up and down the stairs! Useful to have the collections labeled so you can pick and choose based on your interests. Peeked in to see the Rosetta Stone...couldn't say we'd been to the British Museum without at least finding that!

Just in front of the British Museum, good Coffee and better carrot cake.
Very good atention. Thanks 👍

title: Museum Mansion
review_text: British Museum was good and they had arranged the civilization artifacts all and things.

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text: This is an absolute delight to visit, such a  unique little museum brought to life by the star of the show, Gary! Thank you for our time we spent with you, it was truly fascinating and something that we all enjoyed. Highly recommend for those who like a biscuit with their tea!

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text:
I had the incredible opportunity to tour the Peek Frean biscuit company in Bermondsey, London, and explore its fascinating museum. Frank, a former employee, generously shared the rich history and stories behind each relic on display. As a self-proclaimed foodie, I was particularly intrigued by the cream of tomato biscuits, which I wish I could try despite their short-lived existence. The company's innovative tin designs, such as the castle tin that can be connected to build a larger structure, were remarkably ahead of their time. Some tins, like the globe, were more decorative than functional, but highly collectible nonetheless. I was also impressed by the mirror tin, which doubles as a table mirror, and the cleverly designed casket tin that requires a bit of puzzle-solving to open. A humorous highlight was the "naughty tin," created by a disgruntled former employee who expressed their grievances through the design.
Peek Frean had an interesting policy of not hiring married women due to potential Quaker affiliations. Long-serving employees received clocks, while women who got married were given cakes. Overall, the museum is a must-visit for anyone interested in history, food, or design.

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text:
This is a national treasure in the heart of London, Bermondsey. Take a tour with Gary and share the amazing stories of the Peek Freans factory that made biscuits famous around the world. There’s biscuit tins galore, photos to show the range of biscuits that salesmen took around the shops, the most enormous wedding cake replica and when I visited a  cabinet in honour of the Jaffa Cake that promises it is not a biscuit.
Email Gary to book and you are in for a special treat!

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text:
After reading about this gem on londonist.com I fired an email to museum operators Gary and Frank about opening times. Gary came back to me and a few hours later he’d opened up just for us. What a treat! Email addresses are available on the website.

My friend and I spent 2 hours looking around the two rooms, mostly guided passionately by Gary. He knows so much about the history of the building, the company and the personal stories which make this tour so special. He really is an asset. Just a really unique experience I’ve never had anywhere else.

Accessibility is good though some shelves/cabinets may be too high for wheelchair users.

Highlights include a replica of the cake Peek Freans gifted to the Queen, a boatload of buscuit tins and a section dedicated to the war effort. Highly recommended. Free entry.

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text:
A heartfelt thank you to both Gary and Frank for sharing this window into biscuit & London history. An incredibly informative presentation was given by Frank, who has decades of experience working at the factory/ complex when it was in operation. There was then time to explore a huge range of artefacts that paint an unparalleled picture into not only the factory but life more generally for workers during the period. Frank showing me the hidden illustrations, drawn by a disgruntled past employee, that were overlooked and made it onto one of the biscuit tins was a particularly funny highlight. The Jaffa Cake drama was also hilarious to hear.

We were also fortunate enough for Gary to show us some of the sights of Bermondsey with an impromptu historical tour of the area. We particularly enjoyed learning all about the Southwark Park Bandstand and Bermondsey War Memorial. Both Frank and Gary are incredibly knowledgeable and welcoming, and we left feeling an overwhelming sense of gratitude to both for their efforts to conserve, maintain and share the stories surrounding such a unique collection. Thank you both for having us and giving our group such a memorable afternoon!

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text: A thoroughly enjoyable 90 minutes with Frank. He gave us a talk and answered all of our questions. The museum is a real labour of love for Frank and Gary. It's a great little museum - highly recommended

title: Peek Freans Museum
review_text: A small museum with volunteer guides with a long personal connection to the old Peek Freans biscuit factory. Book a group visit.

I love tve British museum its so big and you can spend the whole dayweeks months there and you find something new.
They also have individual exhibitions such as Manga, Edvard Munch, DNA, Terracotta Army, etc its so eclectic.
People go to see the Pantheon/Elgin Marbles but I go to see the large stone moai from Rapa Nui (Easter Island) Hoa Hakananai'a.

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text: Seeing the human history timeline and the level of effort of preservation of the artefacts that the British Museum is just a marvel. A place where not only research groups has access to its rich repository but as a family outing one can instil and be inspired. Hope humanity can preserve for future generations our own civilization better than our predecessors...

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text: There are of course many amazing objects here and fascinating things to learn about. Unfortunately, however, the visitor experience is not very pleasant due to the high volume of people. It’s a struggle to see what’s in the exhibitions or read the info as it’s so crowded. Need the loo? Long queues everywhere. Fancy a coffee or snack? That’ll be £5.50 for a scone and more than £4 for a coffee. Altogether off-putting. Such a shame.

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text: This place is outstanding. World class!! Costs nothing to come in but they ask for a donation which is fair enough. You could spend days in here!

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text:
The British Museum is not just a museum; it's a voyage through human history and a testament to the curiosity and creativity of the human spirit. Whether you're an art aficionado, history buff, or simply a curious traveller, a visit to The British Museum is an opportunity to unlock the treasures of our shared heritage. So, make sure to include it in your London itinerary and let history unfold before your eyes.
The best part? Entry to The British Museum is free. Its central location in London makes it easily accessible by public transport, and guided tours are available for those who want a deeper understanding of the collection.

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text: Not sure what I can say. This is one of the finest museums in the world. Definitely a testament to the age of empire with its vast collection of antiquities from just about every part of the world. Be prepared to spend several days here if you want to see what the museum has to offer. It's simply amazing!!!

title: British Museum (Stop W)
review_text: The Greatest archaeological museum I have visited so far. Though not specialized and having better competitors in the different areas (the Roman museums in Rome and Naples are better for Roman History, the Cretan Museum is better for the Cretan civilization, etc.), this museum encompasses all Ancient Civilizations I am interested in and has gathered millions of findings uncovered by the numerous British expeditions in the Ancient Near East. It took me whole day just to pass quickly the Egyptian, Assyrian, Babylonian, Persian sections. One needs at least a week to cover all interesting objects and to read through the descriptions of the different findings. A place to return numerous times during the lifetime of any enlightened person ! Where else can someone find so grand finds, that have turned the science of mankind history like the Flood Tablet, the Rosetta stone, the palette of Narmer, the Akkadian, Sumerian and Assyrian cuneiform tables and so on and so forth.

title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum
place_title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum
review_text: Enjoyed a trip to the Mail Rail on Thursday 12th February. It was not too busy so we were really pleased. We had to wait about 5 minutes for the train but that was alright as we were able to chat to the guide about the train and museum. I enjoyed the ride and all the interactive displays. My favourite was sorting the mail on the moving train, I couldn't believe how much the simulator moved about!

He also enjoyed the Sorted postal play space.

Definitely highly recommending this Mail Rail experience for your little ones.

review_text: This building is opposite the postal museum and is purely about the royal mail tunnels that lie beneath central London. We found the whole tour very interesting (if a little rushed). The staff were very good, it was well organised and the facilities were excellent. We would recommend the mail rail, although personally, I found the museum opposite more interesting.

title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum
place_title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum
review_text: Absolutely fantastic in every way. The definition of small but mighty museum. So glad they kept the heritage of this place, and didn't repurpose it for something else. A must visit.

title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum

review_text: Really lovely museum to visit with a family,  although also enough of interest for adults. We visited both the mail rail and the main museum (which are just down the road from each other). The website had warned there may be a little wait for the mail rail, but we were probably on about the 3rd train and there was a mini train set for the kids to play with while we waited. The mail rail was done really well and we learned lots about something I'd never really heard of before. The main museum also had lots of interesting exhibits so lots to see and do. It didn't feel mega busy even though we were there in half term.

title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum

title: Mail Rail at The Postal Museum

title: Lord's MCC Cricket Museum
address: Lord's Cricket Ground, St John's Wood Rd, London, NW8 8QN
review_text: I recently had the most incredible experience visiting Lord’s Cricket Stadium in London! I took a guided tour, which lasted about two to two and a half hours, starting with the museum. The museum was a treasure trove of cricket history—so fascinating, especially seeing the origins and evolution of the sport. After that, the guided tour was just amazing. Our guide shared so many captivating stories about iconic events, legendary cricketers, and the milestones achieved here. It truly felt like stepping into the “Mecca of Cricket.” If you’re a cricket fan, this is an absolute must-visit—so insightful and rich with history!

Throughout the tour the guide will take you through the history of the place and history of different cricket teams at the place

title: Lord's MCC Cricket Museum
address: Lord's Cricket Ground, St John's Wood Rd, London, NW8 8QN
review_text: Absolutely a MUST visit for cricket lovers. Easy access when you are here for the match , so do not miss ! I was there on the day Sachin’s portrait was unveiled. The home of the real “Ashes” urn and so many other incredible cricket memorabilia. What a privilege !

title: Lord's MCC Cricket Museum
address: Lord's Cricket Ground, St John's Wood Rd, London, NW8 8QN
review_text: Definitely worth going in while you are there for the cricket, there are lots of interesting items from throughout the history of cricket. There was also some interesting cricket art pieces. It was busy but that was to be expected as it was before the hundred final.

title: Lord's MCC Cricket Museum
address: Lord's Cricket Ground, St John's Wood Rd, London, NW8 8QN
review_text:
The museum is at Lord’s, the home of cricket, at the opposite end of the ground to “Cherie Blair’s Mouth.” It is also the starting point for frequent tours of the historic pavilion and club buildings.

The MCC Museum documents 400 years of cricketing history through paintings, artifacts, photographs, and documents. The museum's pride of place is the tiny, original Ashes urn and the Wisden trophy. Also on display are cricket bats, balls, and kits donated by various players over the years.

The MCC has been the  sole arbiter of the game’s rules since 1788 and the museum displays the oldest existing copy of those rules

Real Ashes Trophy, Prudential Cup (1983 World Cup), Dada (Sourav Ganguly)'s Jersey, T20 World Cup - and N no of stories: all are perfectly stored here.

Fact: Even if Australia wins the Ashes series, a real 10 cm urn remains on display in the MCC museum.

there are many artifacts like signed Bats, oil paintings, trophies, Blazers, and many more are there, but entry to the Museum comes with a Tour of Lord's Ground.

title: Royal Academy of Music Museum
review_text: The piano museum on the top floor is superb, with the added bonus of a window into the Academy's piano workshop. Performances on historic instruments sometimes. Good storyboards about the London piano-making industry. Similar with the strings on the first floor. The museum now has restricted opening hours, so need to check. Toilets, wheelchair access all ok.

review_text: What a charming place to hear some musicians, grab a drink and look at the stunning stained glass windows. It has a full schedule of programmes including opera and a museum. Andrew the front of house manager filled us in on the history.

title: Royal Academy of Music Museum
review_text:
This should be on the bucket list of all music lovers, especially those who love the instrument(s) they play. I spent some fabulous time at the museum with very knowledgeable guides/demonstrators who are talented students of the Academy. Not only did they show me around the strings and keyboard sections, they also shared a lot about their aspirations and dreams. As an amateur pianist myself, I was naturally more drawn to the different harpsichords, clavichord and pianos, but I also discovered interesting things in the strings section, like the piccolo violin and different designs of harps.

To add to the experience, the Academy's reception staff was so friendly. You felt immediately welcome from the get-go. 🙂

Highly recommended! 👍🏻

title: The Gingerbread City by Museum of Architecture
address: Unit 5, Coal Drops Yard, Stable St, London N1C 4DQ
place_title: The Gingerbread City by Museum of Architecture
review_text:
A small Christmastime exhibition that seriously overdelivers on creativity. Gingerbread City at Coal Drops Yard (run by the Museum of Architecture) was packed with wildly inventive builds - from Rubik’s Cubes and moving carousels to treehouses, Big Ben and even Quidditch scenes. The 2025 theme of “Playfulness” really came through, and it was impressive to see what different architecture and design firms come up with in gingerbread form. You could vote for your favourite at the end, which adds a nice interactive touch.

It was spread across two rooms and you can see everything in 15–20 minutes, longer if you stop to read all the descriptions. I’d recommend going earlier in the season if possible - some pieces are delicate and may lose small elements over time if visitors aren’t careful.

Pre-booking recommended (was around £13 per adult in 2025). Pop-up runs late November through December.

Toilets are next door in Coal Drops Yard.

title: The Gingerbread City by Museum of Architecture
address: Unit 5, Coal Drops Yard, Stable St, London N1C 4DQ
place_title: The Gingerbread City by Museum of Architecture
review_text:
Fun annual festival run by a small charity called Museum of Architecture - in its 9th year now, this time at Coal Drops Yard.

About sixty architects have submitted buildings ranging from Big Ben to Harry Potter’s Quidditch stadium - Pui Man’s Olympia is a total showstopper!

Kids will enjoy the fun Treasure Hunt and adults can get lots of tips for decorating the gingerbread houses at home- admire buildings by the offices of Norman Foster and Zaha Hadid - ‘Playful City is the theme this year and there are also fun gingerbread house building workshops available.

This year did not compare well. It looked as if small fingers had knocked off pieces of gingerbread and sweets making some of the exhibits look very tired. It was cramped and with a couple of exceptions the creations lacked the wow factor I was expecting. Perhaps the exhibition is only aimed at children these days. That is how it felt since the exhibits were in reaching distance for many children and looked "nibbled".

The exhibits are amazing, just the design and set up needs more consideration. Won't be visiting again which is a shame as we really enjoyed it 2 years ago.

title: National Army Museum
review_text:
The NAM used to be - in my view - a better museum than the Imperial War Museum. It had fascinating displays with lots of information, in galleries based on the period. Its First World War gallery was truly excellent, as was its Napoleonic one. There were models, uniforms, weapons, equipment, paintings, people's accounts and experiences.

Yes it was a bit aged but it conveyed but it had lots to tell about the Army's story. It was a museum run by people who were fascinated by the British Army and its history.

Since the refurbishment it seems to be much more about concepts, lacking the substance of the older museum. The galleries are confused and one gets the impression that the management is embarrassed by the subject matter of the museum.

The concepts are in some cases barely related to the history of the army. One of the galleries was all about the army's influence on civilian art, with edwardian biscuit tins and modern music posters with a military influence. In one of its more war-like galleries there was an artistic display of various weapons from the War. That is to say, they were arranged in a sort of sculpture.

It begs the question, exactly who are they aiming the museum at? The old museum had an incredible exhibition on reconstructive surgery, and another where you walked down an Afghan alleyway, with hidden IEDs. They were thought provoking and brought a little something of the reality of the wars britain engaged in to civilians. Post revamp, there was an exhibition on Vivian Westwood's anti-war knitting.

Somewhere along the lines they appear to have lost their way and the whole thing was a little disappointing.

title: British Optical Association Museum
review_text:
Many thanks to Dr. Neil Handley, PhD, AMA, FRSA, curator of the British Optical Association Museum, for his warm hospitality. This museum, the world’s oldest dedicated to the vision sciences, was founded in 1901 and houses an impressive collection of over 28,000 notable objects. These include historical visual aids, spectacle frames and lenses, diagnostic equipment, vision tests, and models of eye diseases.

The museum is open to the public and offers guided tours, which must be booked in advance.

Dr. Handley is also the author of "CULT EYEWEAR: The World’s Enduring Classics", an essential reference for eyewear enthusiasts. Experiencing a guided tour led by him and exploring the fascinating curiosities of optometry is, without a doubt, a true privilege. Thank you

title: British Optical Association Museum
review_text: Wonderful tour of this interesting and quirky museum!  Many thanks to Neil the curator, who showed us around and shared many wonderful stories and anecdotes!

review_text: There are thousands of historical optician artefacts with their great stories and interesting facts packed in this tiny hidden gem, conveniently located in central london. The tour included a history of optical evolvement and also offersthe chance to see examples of the first spectacles that takes you through to an exhibit of the queen mother's glasses! This is a free tour, but please make the optional donation requested. I definitely recommend a visit, something really different and an absolute hidden gem.

title: Crime Museum
review_text: I've been meaning to go for years, and then an opportunity suddenly popped up. It was absolutely fascinating. I could happily have spent all day there. The exhibits and history are remarkable. It's easy to see why it's not open to the public, out of respect to victims and due to the highly sensitive nature of many of the exhibits. The Curator has a wealth of knowledge, and was very welcoming. Any officer, former or current, could certainly learn a lot from this very valuable resource. It's a credit to the Met.

title: Crime Museum
review_text:

Meanwhile, there is an exhibit at the old Bow Street Police Station that is open to the public, and which contains a few crime artifacts.

title: Crime Museum
review_text: Fascinating visit where the amount of exhibits on display means you’d need an extra 3 hours at least to get round to everything. From death masks of those hung in the 1840’s to Dennis Nilsen’s bath, any police officer should use the privilege to go and visit it at least once. With the curator being super knowledgeable allowing you to learn even more about the story of the items on display.

title: Crime Museum
review_text:
Recent visit to the museum, must be booked in advance, it isn't open to the public so only Met Police staff, serving or retired officers or officers from other forces making it one of the most exclusive museums in London. No fees to pay, but a voluntary contribution is welcomed.

An excellent visit that any officer should make efforts to see.

Has over 250 exhibits, and I mean exhibits, everything you see, are or were actual exhibits in a criminal court case from many years ago to more recent.

The curator was very friendly, knowledgeable, gives a spoken and guided tour for first time visitors which was a very nice touch.

Afterwards there are guided audio logs if your interested in any particular exhibit.

There are exhibits that have never been released to the public due to their sensitive nature and respect to the victim/s.

You only have 3 hour slots but there so much you'll probably need a second visit.

title: Crime Museum

Had no idea why he had to literally PUSH us out as we still had 15 mins plus we were clearly walking in the direction of the exit.

The venue was laid out with lots of small areas, old church pews and quaint seating and so much memorabilia to examine - both on the walls and in cabinets and displays.

Lots of posters of great comedians, books, nik-naks, larger ornaments and models.

On a slightly negative side, perhaps, the venue is a little dark and is perhaps a little small for big parties, because of the layout (check recommended numbers with the venue).

It's definitely different and makes for an interesting place to visit. Nearest tube: Holborn

title: Museum Superstore
review_text:
Extremely disappointing and discriminatory experience

I visited this store with my friends, and as a woman of color, I was racially profiled by a staff member. He asked me to wait even though I hadn’t purchased anything, then proceeded to check the security cameras in front of me. After confirming I hadn’t done anything wrong, he told me I could leave.

He then went to my friend, who was still shopping, and continued to check the cameras with her present, telling other customers that he was “sure” I had stolen something and that he had been working there for years, so he was confident about his accusation.

When I went back inside to get my friend, he apologized and was smiling, insisting that he “just had to check” and “had to be sure.”

title: Museum Superstore
review_text:
I was shopping at this store with my friend, who is a woman of color. While I was still browsing, a staff member stopped her and asked her to wait, even though she hadn’t bought anything. He then went to check the security cameras right in front of her. After confirming she hadn’t done anything wrong, he told her she could leave.

As she left, the same staff member came to me while I was still shopping and continued checking the cameras, saying to other customers that he was “sure” my friend had stolen something and that he had been working there for years, so he could “tell.”

I was shocked and uncomfortable. When my friend came back to get me, he suddenly changed his tone — smiling and apologizing, saying he “just had to check” and “had to be sure.” The whole situation was humiliating and clearly racially motivated.

To make matters worse, I was offensively asked to open my bag as if I were suspected of something upon leaving?! There were no clear signs indicating this policy, and the manner in which it was requested was rude and accusatory.

Definitely not a place I’ll be returning to, and I wouldn’t recommend it to others. There are many other souvenir or gift shops that respect their customers and provide honest service.

title: Astor Museum Hostel
review_text: It’s a brilliantly located hostel. However, as other reviewers have commented, I’m surprised at this hostel’s high rating. Because although the staff here are super friendly and accommodating, everything else is poor.||||Beds don’t have privacy curtains, which in today’s world, is pretty unforgivable. Especially when the dorms can be so hot that guests sleep on top of their bedding with little clothing!||I moved to a different dorm during my stay, both were very noisy and very warm (during good weather). They are no hooks to hang anything including wet towels, so dorms are messy.||||The bathroom for my first dorm was small, old and felt dirty. By contrast, the bathroom for my second dorm was modern and clean - world’s apart!||||The entire property isn’t particularly clean, partly because they rely on the live-in staff to do the cleaning, rather than using a proper cleaning company like most other hostels do. The staff are lovely, but they are not professional cleaners. ||||There’s a real lack of communal space to have some alone time, or do some work. The TV room is small, leaving only the kitchen area, which is always busy. Also, annoyingly the kitchen must be vacated mid-morning (at exactly when guests will want to make breakfast) so that the staff can clean it - bad decision!||||The laundry situation was dire, with one machine, that was partly broken. ||||I loved the staff, so I wish this hostel was better, but it’s pretty terrible. There are MUCH better London hostels for your money.

title: Astor Museum Hostel
review_text:
This hostel is more than suitable for a short stay with good transport access. I stayed 4 nights in a 4-bed female dorm, and while it’s not the nicest one I’ve been to, it provided me with the bare minimum of a clean bed to rest my head. Additionally, having a basin with a soap dispenser in my room was very convenient as I didn’t have to worry about waiting to use a sink in the communal bathrooms. Staff are friendly and helpful. Laundry detergent pods sold for £1 at the front desk. Bathrooms were relatively clean, but shower curtains felt a bit unhygienic.

One of my biggest criticisms, however, is the laundry situation which is pretty dire, so I strongly suggest you to do your laundry elsewhere as there are only two washer-dryers and it was almost a competition to try and get your washing load in before someone else did. While not the fault of the hostel, it also didn’t help that some people wouldn’t pick up their laundry up in a timely manner so clothes would be left in the dryer for hours leaving you waiting for longer to use the machines. It’s also behind a door in a hallway that blocks access to people entering or leaving from the kitchen behind it. I’ve included a photo of it for reference as there don’t seem to be any photos on their website.

Wifi was pretty patchy too and often forced me to burn up a lot of my mobile data. Power sockets near my bed didn’t work. Also no lift/elevator (like many hostels), so don’t bring any massive bags or suitcases with you unless you’re prepared to drag them up several flights of stairs if you’re assigned a top level room.

review_text: Awesome experience. Love the virtual tour. Amazing architecture. The Crown jewels are truly something to behold, just mind blowing. Also loved touring the armory. "Can you build something that will last 1000 years".

title: Tower of London
review_text:
Absolutely incredible experience at the Tower of London!

The history alone is mind-blowing… centuries of royalty, intrigue, betrayal, mystery, and unforgettable stories all wrapped into one iconic place. You can truly feel the weight of the past as you walk through the grounds.

And of course… the ravens 🐦‍⬛ stole my heart. Seeing them up close (and learning about the legend that protects the kingdom!) was such a unique and memorable part of the visit.

If you go, do not skip the Beefeater tour. It is 100% worth it! Ours was funny, engaging, and full of fascinating stories you’d never pick up just wandering on your own. The mix of humor and history made it both entertaining and educational.

One of the best historical attractions I’ve visited… immersive, atmospheric, and absolutely unforgettable.

Highly recommend!

title: Tower of London
review_text:
One of London's largest and most impressive visitor attractions, the Tower of London is not to be missed. Whether you've got limited time and just walk around the perimeter walls taking in the magnificent architecture, or have a few hours to spend for a full visit, there's plenty to see at this World Heritage Site.
Occupying a strategic position overlooking the Thames, the instantly recognisable White Tower is one of the city's finest Norman buildings. Inside, it houses exhibits covering the Tower's history, supported by well presented collections of artefacts. The Tower is guarded by the famous ravens that still inhabit the grounds today.
Other areas of the site worth exploring include the Battlements, Medieval Palace with period rooms, and exhibitions showcasing the Crown Jewels and historic punishments used at the Tower. The attractive buildings of Tower Green and the Chapel Royal of St Peter ad Vincula are amongst the site's architectural highlights.
Photography is allowed in most areas excluding the Crown Jewels exhibition. Guided tours are provided at intervals throughout the day, and a basic free audio tour is available on smartphones if you prefer to guide yourself.
Onsite facilities are comprehensive and include cafes offering good lunch options and predictably expensive refreshments, gift shops and toilets. The nearest station is Tower Hill, a few minutes walk away.
At the time of our visit, adult ticket prices were £37. It's expensive, but not excessive for a large site that's presented to a very high standard. Annual memberships can work out more cost effective if you're planning to make return visits within the year. It's a very popular site and gets busy, so it's advisable to buy tickets online in advance to minimise waiting times.

title: Tower of London
review_text:
Really enjoyed our time here. We did a self-guided visit and booked tickets online beforehand, which I would definitely recommend. There were queues but everything was well organised and we got in quickly.
Set aside about three hours if you want to see everything properly. What makes it special is how the atmosphere changes as you walk through different sections representing various periods of history. It really brings the place to life.
We arrived early on a Sunday morning and it was already fairly busy. It only gets more crowded as the day goes on, so an early start is worth it.
Staff were friendly and happy to help whenever we had questions. A fantastic attraction that works for solo travellers, couples and families alike. Highly recommended.

title: Tower of London
review_text:

It houses the famous Crown Jewels and many historic towers and walls.
The place feels immersive, with stories of kings, prisoners, and ancient events.
The site is well maintained and guided tours are very informative.
Overall, it’s an unforgettable experience for history lovers and visitors.

title: Museum Tavern
review_text:
Book a table at the Museum Tavern & select an entree other than traditional eats as they can prepare foods very well to deserve 5 stars for pub dining.
Across from British Museum this historic pub has everything you would look for; atmosphere, chatter, charm, outdoor dining with gorgeous hanging plants, great service that are understaffed & still doing a top-notch job.
We were completely impressed by the Duck Hash as recommended by server, his fav dish. It was one of the best meals we’ve had in days.
The price was half for what you’d pay in any NE USA city & tasted twice better.
A cheeseburger was 2 pounds less so I say go for the best experience while you can. Make the burger at home.
Restrooms down a flight so it’s not handicap accessible for that reason. But main entrance is one step up.
A few floorboards are mushy & need replacement before breaking thru.
Recommend a fine lunch experience here with friendly staff.

title: Museum Tavern
review_text:
There are pub roasts, and then there are roasts. The kind that make you seriously consider cancelling the rest of your afternoon just so you can sit in a gravy-induced trance. That’s what we found last weekend at the Museum Tavern.

We kicked things off with a couple of pints—lager and Guinness, obviously—because it’s Sunday and rules are rules.

Starters were a pleasant surprise: a fresh, punchy feta salad and a salmon scotch egg that tasted like someone in the kitchen actually cares. Properly seasoned, nicely done—more “quality restaurant” than “random pub corner starter,” which is a rare and beautiful thing.

Then came the roast beef. Tender, still blushing in the middle, and piled high with everything you want: grilled cabbage, sweet carrots, cheesy cauliflower (which deserves its own applause), roast potatoes that crunched properly, a flood of rich, savoury gravy—and yes, a Yorkshire pudding. Puffy, golden, and there like it belonged, not like it wandered in by accident.

It was one of those meals that hits the sweet spot: comforting, well-executed, no fuss, no flops. We left happy, full, and just slightly more optimistic about life. A proper Sunday roast done right—and yes, worth the second pint.

title: Museum Tavern

title: Museum Tavern
review_text:
After more than two hours wandering through the endless halls of the British Museum, we were starving – and thankfully, we didn’t have to walk far. Directly opposite waits The Museum Tavern, a proper London pub with history in its walls and beer in its veins.

By now, fish & chips and burgers had been our standard pub companions, but here we decided to try something different. The steak pie turned out to be a revelation: hearty, rich, and exactly what you need after too much culture and not enough food. Paired with perfectly poured pints, it was the fuel we didn’t know we needed.

The beer selection deserves special mention – wide enough that we never had to drink the same beer twice. Each round brought a new taste, each pint a new highlight.

The Museum Tavern proves once again that London pubs aren’t just about eating and drinking – they’re part of the journey. After history on the museum’s walls, we found history in a glass, and a new dish to add to our pub tour memory: steak pie forever.

title: Museum Tavern

Very friendly and helpful staff, arrived early for our booking and sat outside enjoying the sunshine until our table was available. Was seated promptly and food, which arrived in good time, was delicious - can definitely recommend the steak and ale pie. 😋

Definitely worth booking in advance as not particularly large venue. Not necessarily a bad thing though as it allows them to focus on quality over quantity.

title: Garden Museum Venue Hire
review_text:
We had the most incredible wedding at the Garden Museum, and we truly couldn’t imagine a more gorgeous venue. From the moment we first stepped inside, we knew it was something special - the atmosphere, the architecture, the gardens, everything felt magical.

The entire team at the Garden Museum were an absolute dream to work with. Everyone was warm, friendly, and genuinely invested in helping us create the best day possible. The Head of Commercial Events (Jessie Clifford) and the Garden Museum Cafe Supervisor (Sam Chester) were so kind and organised; they made every stage of the process feel easy and enjoyable. They answered my many emails (and there were many!!) provided brilliant supplier recommendations (even down to our cake), and always kept us calm and reassured. By the time we came in for our final run-through, they had everything prepared so thoroughly that any nerves disappeared - we knew we were in the safest hands.
On the day itself, the whole staff worked seamlessly together to make sure everything ran perfectly. The wedding went off without a single hitch, and we were able to be completely present and enjoy every moment because we trusted the team so much. Our guests even commented on how beautifully smooth and well-organised the day felt.
Overall, the Garden Museum and its wonderful team gave us a wedding day we will remember forever. The venue is stunning, the staff are lovely, and their care and professionalism made all the difference. We couldn’t recommend them more highly. 5*!!

title: Garden Museum Venue Hire
review_text: We had our wedding lunch at the Garden Museum (Clore Learning Space for 18 people). Jessie, Maria and the whole team were amazing. Definitely recommend for bespoke venue hire. Jessie and Maria were both very responsive to requests. All the guests commented on how lovely the venue was and that the food was super tasty.

title: Garden Museum Venue Hire
review_text:
We held our wedding reception at the Garden Museum (October 2023), and could not have dreamed up a more beautiful, magical evening. We were truly blown away by how wonderfully everything and everyone came together. As we were planning our wedding from California, we considered hiring a wedding coordinator/planner in London, and ultimately found that it was not necessary for us once we decided to have our reception at the Garden Museum. Their highly curated list of trusted suppliers made finding vendors who fit with our aesthetic much easier than anticipated, and the support that our events manager (Jessie Clifford) gave us throughout the planning process was invaluable. Her prompt communication, guidance, and willingness to work with us to accommodate what we envisioned really helped bring our day together, in addition to giving us the peace of mind that we were in good hands! We had a few last minute changes in the days leading up to our reception, and Jessie handled/resolved each issue impeccably.

Most of our guests (British and American) had never heard of or been to the Garden Museum before, and we were thrilled to share such a hidden gem with our nearest and dearest. Over a month later, and we are still getting compliments on what a beautiful venue it was, how the day - from start to finish - ran seamlessly (despite speeches going on a bit longer than expected!), and how helpful and kind museum staff and vendors were.

We highly recommend the Garden Museum for anyone - in the UK and abroad - planning a wedding reception, but really, any life event or celebration!

title: Garden Museum Venue Hire
review_text:
I am a Wedding Planner and worked with the Garden Museum events team and their suppliers to produce my couples wedding at this beautiful venue in January 2024.

The Garden Museum is very much a blank canvas venue and it allowed for lots of creativity when designing this wedding. My bride and groom opted for a Winter Woodland walk theme and the venue was transformed by the suppliers.

Jessie and the Garden Museum team were really great at responding to emails quickly, answering questions on what was and wasn't permitted, checking measurements and making recommendations on what would and wouldn't work well. They also proactively kept me up to date with what museum items would be in place and letting us know what items would be moved. Once we had the pre event meeting with suppliers, the GM team were very flexible with the access and delivery times to ensure the wedding would be set and ready for guest arrival which was peace of mind for myself and my clients.

On the day of the wedding the GM team including Jessie, the Venue Manager, were really accommodating to requests and ensuring the wedding day ran smoothly, nothing was too much trouble. All the staff delivered the service with a smile on their face and seemed genuinely happy to help and be there making the wedding day event better for the wedding couple. The venue looked absolutely beautiful and the feedback from the wedding couple and guests has been fantastic, a wedding they will never forget.

I have worked in lots of venues throughout my career and can confirm that the Garden Museum is one of the best in London. 5 star hospitality and stunningly beautiful. They also have a fantastic supplier list who know the venue really well and help to ensure everything ran smoothly. If you are considering booking the Garden Museum for your wedding, I would certainly recommend you do so!
